journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Concentrations of antimicrobial components in milk of Japanese Black cows and their differences from dairy cows

contrib-group.aff: 1)Graduate School of Integrated Sciences for Life, Hiroshima University, Hiroshima, Japan
2)Joint Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Kagoshima University, Kagoshima, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Isobe N: niso@hiroshima-u.ac.jp, Graduate School of Integrated Sciences for Life, Hiroshima University, 1-4-4 Kagamiyama, Higashi-Hiroshima, Hiroshima 739-8528, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2024 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p:
This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0:
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: The components of milk from beef cows remain to be elucidated. This study examined the differences in the antimicrobial components of milk between dairy and beef cows. Quarter milk was collected from both Japanese Black (beef type) and Holstein (dairy type) cows to compare the concentrations of antimicrobial components. The concentration of lingual antimicrobial peptide (LAP) was higher, whereas that of the other antimicrobial components (lactoferrin, S100A7, and S100A8) was lower in beef cows than in dairy cows. Overall, these results indicate that the differences in antimicrobial components between beef and dairy cows may be associated with the difference in the prevalence of mastitis between them.

body.p: Despite several studies examining the components of milk from beef cattle [13, 19], their immune components remain unknown. Notably, mastitis is a major problem in dairy cows but not in beef cows. Mastitis is thought to occur frequently in dairy cows because milking causes intramammary infection [15]; however, beef cows are also thought to have an opportunity for intramammary infection, especially by environmental pathogens through suckling and teat contact with cow bedding [1, 2]. Therefore, the difference in the frequency of mastitis between dairy cows and beef cows may be due in part to differences in the immune function of the udder. We previously reported that the concentrations of some antimicrobial components in the milk of dairy cows are lower in summer, when environmental mastitis occurs frequently [18]. Particularly, innate immunity contributes to prevent udder infections; however, this aspect has not been examined in beef cattle.

Antimicrobial components are important factors of innate immunity. Several antimicrobial components are found in the udder, and these play a role in the immunological defense system. For example, in cattle, lingual antimicrobial peptide (LAP) is synthesized by mammary epithelial cells and secreted into the milk [7]. LAP is the secondary defense that works after the physical barrier to prevent intramammary infection [3, 7, 21]. Lactoferrin (LF), synthesized by mammary epithelial cells and leukocytes, absorbs iron and exhibit bacteriostatic activity [16]. The milk LF concentration is important to prevent environmental mastitis during dry period [20], and summer season when cows are affected by heat stress [18]. The S100A proteins are Ca-binding proteins; of these, S100A7 is secreted from teat epithelia, whereas S100A8 is secreted by leukocytes [11, 22]. S100A7 show antimicrobial activity especially against Escherichia coli, the representative pathogen which is present in cow beds and causes environmental mastitis [14]. As these antimicrobial proteins and peptides are secreted into non-inflamed normal milk to kill invading bacteria [18]. Further, when the mammary glands are inflamed in mastitis, the concentration of these antimicrobial components in dairy milk is increased to eliminate mastitis-causing pathogens [6, 17]. Thus, antimicrobial components such as LF, LAP and S100 proteins in milk play an important role in preventing mammary gland infections in dairy cows. However, their presence and concentrations in beef cattle remain to be determined.

In this study, the antimicrobial component concentrations in the milk from beef cows and those from dairy cows were measured and compared. These findings can provide insights into why the incidence of mastitis varies greatly cattle breed, especially between dairy and beef cattle.

This study included 35 lactating Japanese Black beef cows raised on a farm in Kagoshima Prefecture, Japan, and 10 Holstein Friesian dairy cows raised on a farm at Hiroshima University. As the concentration of antimicrobial components increases within 1 week after calving, samples were collected after 1 week postpartum (that is, 1 to 8 weeks after calving for beef cows and 40 weeks after calving for dairy cows). Milk was collected from two quarters (the front left and rear left udders) for beef cows and from all four quarters for dairy cows. This study was conducted in accordance with the guidelines for animal experiments issued by Hiroshima University (E19-3).

As somatic cell count (SCC) in beef cow could not be measured because there was no measuring equipment on the farm and the lab was far from the farm, milk from beef cows was stored at −20°C after collection and thawed to measure the SCC. It was then centrifuged (1,750 × g, 15 min, 4°C) and the resulting skimmed milk was frozen at −20°C until used for further analysis. SCC was measured in milk from dairy cows immediately after collection. The milk was then centrifuged, and the resulting skimmed milk was stored at −20°C until further analysis. To determine the effect of freezing on SCC, the SCC measured immediately after collection and after freeze and thaw were compared using 15 milk samples.

The SCC was determined using an automated cell counter (DeLaval Cell Counter DCC; DeLaval, Tumba, Sweden). The sodium ion (Na+) concentration was measured using a LAQUAtwin Na-11 pocket meter (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). Competitive ELISA was performed to measure the levels of lingual antimicrobial peptide (LAP), S100A7, and S100A8, as previously reported [7, 12, 22]. Milk LF was measured with the sandwich method using a commercial kit (Bethyl Laboratories Inc., Montgomery, TX, USA; Suzuki et al., 2022). S100A8 concentrations in milk of 23 and 12 samples in beef and dairy cows were measured because we were not able to prepare the amount of skim milk necessary for measurement.

Statistical analysis was performed by JMP Pro 16 (SAS Institute, Inc., Cary, NC, USA). The differences of the concentrations of antimicrobial components in milk between dairy and beef cow were analyzed using the Wilcoxon method. The correlation between SCC and each concentration of antimicrobial components were analyzed with Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient. Differences were considered statistically significant at P<0.05.

The mean ± standard deviation of log10 SCC in raw and freeze-thawed milk were 5.22 ± 0.67 and 5.41 ± 0.65, and there were no significantly difference between two groups. The mean ± standard deviation of log10 SCC in milk of beef and dairy cow were 4.98 ± 0.60 and 4.61 ± 0.53 and the SCC values of the two breeds were not significantly different. As the levels of Na+ and antimicrobial components were reported to be higher in the mastitic milk than those in healthy milk, low-SCC milk (<300,000 cells/mL) samples alone were used for further analysis. No difference was observed in Na+ concentration of milk between the two breeds (Fig. 1bFig. 1.
Somatic cell count (SCC) in milk from dairy and beef cows (a) and Na+ concentration in milk with low SCC. Hol: Holstein Friesian, JB: Japanese Black.). However, the LAP concentration was significantly higher in the milk from beef cows than in that from dairy cows (P=0.0004). Conversely, significantly lower concentrations of LF, S100A7, and S100A8 were observed in milk from beef cows than in the milk from dairy cows. SCC and LAP, lactoferrin, S100A7 and S100A8 concentration showed a significant correlation (r=0.38, 0.36, −0.39 and −0.36, respectively) in the milk samples from beef cows as well as those from dairy cows.

In dairy cows, the threshold SCC for mastitis is 300,000 cells/mL; in severe cases of mastitis, the SCC can reach 100 million cells/mL or higher [4, 10]. However, few studies have determined the SCC in milk from beef cows, and the actual SCC values were unclear. In this study, the SCC in milk from beef cows ranged from 11,000 to 2,626,000 cells/mL, which was greater than that in the milk from dairy cows (3,000 to 500,000 cells/mL). The milk from dairy cow used in this study had a relatively low SCC because no cows with clinical mastitis were included in the study. As the clinical signs in beef cows were not examined, an appropriate comparison of the SCC results between the milk from dairy and beef cows in this study is challenging.

The LAP concentrations were significantly higher in the milk from beef cows than in the milk from dairy cow (Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Concentration of lingual antimicrobial peptide (LAP) (a), lactoferrin (b), S100A7 (c), and S100A8 (d) in milk from dairy and beef cows. Hol: Holstein Friesian, JB: Japanese Black.). Lower milk production in beef cows than that in dairy cows may be responsible for the observed higher LAP concentrations. Such an increase in the concentration of antimicrobial components owing to lower milk yields has also been described in other studies [12]. Interestingly, even the milk from healthy dairy cows contains a certain concentration of LAP; however, this concentration has been reported to vary widely among individuals [9]. Although the milk with a low SCC content alone was used in this study, large differences in milk LAP concentrations were observed among individual beef cows. The correlation between SCC and LAP and lactoferrin concentrations was significant for all milk samples from beef cows. Notably, dairy cows with high LAP and lactoferrin concentrations in milk have been reported to be less susceptible to mastitis [8, 20]. This may also be the case for beef cows. As beef cows are less susceptible to mastitis than dairy cows and LAP and lactoferrin may be important in preventing inflammation, beef cows may be protected from bacterial infections by high LAP concentrations in their milk.

Notably, the concentrations of LF, S100A7, and S100A8 in beef cow milk were significantly lower than those in dairy cow milk. LF is an antimicrobial component secreted by mammary epithelial cells [5], S100A7 is secreted by teat epithelial cells [22], and S100A8 is secreted by leukocytes [11]. In a previous study, their concentrations of S100A7 and S100A8 have been reported to increase on the day after stimulation with viable bacteria and bacterial components, whereas the levels of LF increase after a few days. In this study, the concentrations of these antimicrobial components in beef cow were lower than that in dairy cows, regardless of the lack of mastitis severity. This suggests that the udder immune function of beef cows may be less dependent on these antimicrobial components. Alternatively, as the levels of antimicrobial components increase with inflammation [6], even under low SCC conditions, beef cows may be more exposed to reduced inflammation compared with dairy cows. S100A7, secreted by the teat epithelium, is important for protection in the route of bacterial entry. Beef calves are suckled for approximately two months, whereas dairy calves are not suckled even immediately after calving. Calf saliva contains the same antimicrobials as those in milk (unpublished data), and suckling brings saliva in contact with the teats of the mother cow. It may thus play an antimicrobial role around the teats and help eliminate the bacteria attached to the teats. Therefore, an increase in the S100A7 concentration in milk may not be needed in beef cows.

Overall, these results indicate that milk SCC is relatively high in both beef and dairy cows. However, significant differences were observed in the antimicrobial component concentrations between the milk from dairy and beef cows; these differences may be related to the low severity of mastitis in beef cows. These data will be useful for udder management in the future.

sec.title: POTENTIAL Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest associated with this study.

ack.p: The authors thank Rin Furukido (an undergraduate student at Hiroshima University) and the farm staff of Hiroshima University for their cooperation in collecting milk from dairy cows, as well as the farmers who provided milk from beef cows.
